name: Research Lab / PI Group
summary: Principal investigator leads a lab of postdocs, grad students, and staff pursuing a coherent research agenda.
description: The PI Group is the fundamental unit of academic research. A principal investigator secures grant funding and sets the research direction. Postdoctoral researchers conduct independent studies within the lab's scope. Graduate students apprentice under the PI and postdocs, progressing from assigned problems toward independent research. A lab manager handles equipment, procurement, and institutional compliance. The PI balances mentorship, grant writing, teaching, and their own research. Knowledge transfer happens through weekly lab meetings, one-on-one mentorship, and shared lab notebooks. The structure is inherently temporal: students graduate, postdocs move on, and institutional memory resides primarily in publications and the PI's continuity.
realWorldExample: A computational neuroscience lab at MIT where the PI holds an NIH R01 grant. Three PhD students work on related but distinct thesis projects, two postdocs lead sub-teams, and a lab manager maintains the computing cluster and handles IRB protocols. Weekly lab meetings rotate presenters, and the PI meets each student individually biweekly.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs a single visionary leader who mentors junior agents through long-term projects, with knowledge accumulating across generations of participants and funding cycles.
strengths: Deep expertise develops through years-long apprenticeship under an expert
PI provides coherent long-term vision that survives individual departures
Lab meetings create regular knowledge sharing and peer feedback
Small size enables tight mentorship and rapid idea iteration
weaknesses: Single point of failure: if the PI leaves or loses funding, the lab dissolves
Power asymmetry between PI and students can suppress dissent or novel ideas
Institutional memory loss when experienced members graduate or depart
PI becomes bottleneck when juggling mentorship, grants, teaching, and research

agents.name: Principal Investigator
agents.description: Sets research direction, secures funding, mentors all lab members, and represents the lab externally

agents.name: Senior Postdoctoral Researcher
agents.description: Leads independent research threads, co-mentors graduate students, and prepares for faculty positions

agents.name: Junior Postdoctoral Researcher
agents.description: Conducts focused research within the lab agenda, publishes papers, and builds an independent research identity

agents.name: Senior PhD Student
agents.description: Works on thesis research with increasing independence, mentors junior students, and prepares for dissertation

agents.name: Junior PhD Student
agents.description: Learns research methods, takes courses, explores topics under close PI and postdoc supervision

agents.name: Lab Manager
agents.description: Handles equipment maintenance, procurement, safety compliance, IRB protocols, and onboarding of new members

agents.name: Undergraduate Research Assistant
agents.description: Performs directed tasks like data collection, coding, or literature review under graduate student supervision

memoryArchitecture.description: The PI maintains a persistent shared vision document and grant portfolio that defines the lab's direction. Each researcher keeps private experiment logs and thesis drafts. Lab notebooks are append-only shared records. Publications become the permanent institutional memory. When students graduate, their codebases and datasets are archived in shared storage, but tacit knowledge often departs with them.

diagram:
graph TD
    PI[Principal Investigator] --> PS[Senior Postdoc]
    PI --> PJ[Junior Postdoc]
    PI --> LM[Lab Manager]
    PS --> PhD1[Senior PhD Student]
    PS --> PhD2[Junior PhD Student]
    PJ --> PhD2
    PhD1 --> UG[Undergrad RA]
    LM -->|equipment & compliance| PS
    LM -->|equipment & compliance| PJ

name: Peer Review Network
summary: Editors route manuscripts to anonymous reviewers whose evaluations determine publication worthiness.
description: The peer review network is the epistemic immune system of science. Authors submit manuscripts to a journal, where an editor-in-chief triages them to associate editors with domain expertise. The associate editor selects 2-4 anonymous reviewers who evaluate the work for correctness, novelty, and significance. In double-blind review, neither authors nor reviewers know each other's identities. Reviewers submit independent reports, and the associate editor synthesizes them into an accept/revise/reject decision. Authors may revise and resubmit, restarting the cycle. The system relies on voluntary reviewer labor and editor judgment, creating tensions between thoroughness, speed, and fairness.
realWorldExample: A Nature submission goes through the editor-in-chief's desk rejection filter, then to an associate editor who invites five reviewers (expecting three to accept). After eight weeks, three reviews return: one enthusiastic, one critical, one mixed. The associate editor weighs the reviews, requests major revisions, and the authors have 60 days to respond with a revised manuscript and point-by-point rebuttal.
whenToUse: When an agent system must evaluate outputs for quality using independent anonymous assessors, with editorial synthesis to handle disagreement among evaluators.
strengths: Anonymity reduces bias from reputation, status, or institutional prestige
Independent parallel reviews catch errors that any single reviewer would miss
Editor synthesis resolves conflicting reviews with domain judgment
Iterative revision cycles allow work to improve rather than face binary accept/reject
weaknesses: Relies on unpaid voluntary labor, causing reviewer fatigue and slow turnaround
Anonymity can shield lazy or hostile reviews from accountability
Editor subjectivity in synthesizing conflicting reviews introduces inconsistency
Gatekeeping can suppress unconventional ideas that challenge reviewer paradigms

agents.name: Editor-in-Chief
agents.description: Triages incoming submissions, assigns associate editors, and makes final publication decisions on contested cases

agents.name: Associate Editor
agents.description: Selects reviewers, manages the review timeline, synthesizes reviews into editorial decisions, and communicates with authors

agents.name: Reviewer A
agents.description: Independently evaluates manuscript for scientific rigor, novelty, and clarity; submits a structured report with recommendation

agents.name: Reviewer B
agents.description: Provides a second independent evaluation focusing on methodology and reproducibility

agents.name: Reviewer C
agents.description: Provides a third independent evaluation, often selected for complementary expertise to other reviewers

agents.name: Corresponding Author
agents.description: Submits the manuscript, responds to reviewer comments with revisions and rebuttals, and handles post-acceptance proofs

private: author identities (hidden from reviewers in double-blind)
memoryArchitecture.description: The system is defined by information barriers. Reviewers cannot see each other's reports until after submission. Authors cannot see reviewer identities. The associate editor is the only agent who sees all information and synthesizes it. The manuscript itself is versioned: each revision is a new version with tracked changes. Decision letters are append-only records that accumulate across revision rounds.

diagram:
graph TD
    AU[Author] -->|submits| EIC[Editor-in-Chief]
    EIC -->|assigns| AE[Associate Editor]
    AE -->|invites| R1[Reviewer A]
    AE -->|invites| R2[Reviewer B]
    AE -->|invites| R3[Reviewer C]
    R1 -->|report| AE
    R2 -->|report| AE
    R3 -->|report| AE
    AE -->|decision| AU
    AU -->|revision| AE

name: Think Tank
summary: Policy researchers produce analysis that communications teams translate into public influence and funder deliverables.
description: A think tank bridges the gap between academic research and political action. Senior fellows and policy researchers produce in-depth analysis on specific issues. A communications team translates dense research into accessible policy briefs, op-eds, and media appearances. A development team cultivates relationships with funders whose priorities shape the research agenda, creating a tension between intellectual independence and financial sustainability. The executive director manages this tension while setting organizational strategy. Unlike universities, think tanks optimize for policy impact rather than academic rigor, leading to faster publication cycles but greater vulnerability to ideological capture.
realWorldExample: The Brookings Institution employs over 100 resident fellows organized into programs like Foreign Policy and Economic Studies. A senior fellow researches trade policy for months, then works with the communications team to produce a 2-page policy brief timed to a congressional hearing. The development office ensures the fellow's work aligns with foundation grant deliverables. The fellow testifies before Congress, citing the full report.
whenToUse: When an agent system must translate deep analysis into actionable recommendations for decision-makers, balancing research quality with accessibility and timeliness.
strengths: Faster research-to-impact pipeline than traditional academia
Communications layer makes expert knowledge accessible to policymakers
Fellows have freedom to pursue long-term research agendas between publication cycles
Convening power brings together researchers, policymakers, and practitioners
weaknesses: Funder influence can bias research toward predetermined conclusions
Policy relevance pressure may compromise methodological rigor
Revolving door between think tanks and government creates conflicts of interest
Communications simplification can distort nuanced research findings

agents.name: Executive Director
agents.description: Sets organizational strategy, manages the tension between intellectual independence and funder relationships, and represents the think tank publicly

agents.name: Senior Fellow
agents.description: Conducts deep policy research, publishes reports and papers, testifies before legislative bodies, and mentors junior researchers

agents.name: Research Fellow
agents.description: Supports senior fellows with data analysis, literature reviews, and drafting sections of larger reports

agents.name: Communications Director
agents.description: Translates research into policy briefs, op-eds, social media content, and media talking points; books fellows for media appearances

agents.name: Development Officer
agents.description: Cultivates funder relationships, writes grant proposals, ensures research deliverables satisfy grant requirements

agents.name: Events Coordinator
agents.description: Organizes panel discussions, congressional briefings, and public forums that connect researchers with policymakers

private: funder relationship details and grant amounts
memoryArchitecture.description: Published reports form the shared institutional memory and brand. Fellows maintain private research in progress to protect pre-publication findings. The development office keeps funder relationships and financial details private. Communications maintains a shared media contact database and brand guidelines. Institutional positions on key issues are versioned as they evolve over time.

diagram:
graph TD
    ED[Executive Director] --> SF[Senior Fellow]
    ED --> CD[Comms Director]
    ED --> DO[Development Officer]
    SF --> RF[Research Fellow]
    SF -->|research| CD
    CD -->|briefs & media| PUB[Public & Policymakers]
    DO -->|funding| ED
    DO -->|deliverables| SF
    EC[Events Coordinator] --> SF
    EC --> PUB

name: Cross-Disciplinary Research Center
summary: Researchers hold joint appointments across departments, bridging disciplinary silos through shared projects.
description: A cross-disciplinary research center exists as an overlay on traditional departmental structures. Faculty hold joint appointments, belonging to both their home department and the center. A center director coordinates across departments but typically lacks hiring or tenure authority, which remains with departments. This creates a matrix structure where researchers report to both a department chair and the center director. The center provides shared resources like specialized equipment, seed grants, and administrative support that incentivize cross-departmental collaboration. Boundary-spanning postdocs and research staff physically co-located in the center serve as translators between disciplinary languages and methods.
realWorldExample: The Santa Fe Institute operates as a pure cross-disciplinary center where physicists, biologists, economists, and computer scientists collaborate on complexity science. Resident faculty have no departmental affiliations; external faculty hold appointments at their home universities. Working groups convene for weeks to tackle specific problems, mixing disciplines deliberately. The Institute's flat structure and shared lunches force daily cross-pollination.
whenToUse: When an agent system must integrate specialized agents from different domains to solve problems that no single domain can address alone, requiring translation between different frameworks and vocabularies.
strengths: Bridges disciplinary silos that traditional departmental structures enforce
Shared physical and computational resources lower barriers to collaboration
Joint appointments give researchers access to multiple knowledge networks
Boundary-spanning roles actively translate between disciplinary languages
weaknesses: Dual reporting to department and center creates loyalty and priority conflicts
Center director often lacks hiring and tenure authority, limiting strategic control
Disciplinary depth may suffer when researchers spread across multiple communities
Success metrics are unclear: departments value publications, centers value collaboration

agents.name: Center Director
agents.description: Coordinates cross-disciplinary research agenda, allocates seed grants, and mediates between departmental priorities

agents.name: Faculty Member (Dept A)
agents.description: Holds joint appointment; brings disciplinary expertise from Department A to center projects

agents.name: Faculty Member (Dept B)
agents.description: Holds joint appointment; brings complementary expertise from Department B

agents.name: Faculty Member (Dept C)
agents.description: Holds joint appointment; brings a third disciplinary perspective to collaborative problems

agents.name: Boundary-Spanning Postdoc
agents.description: Co-located in the center; translates between disciplinary vocabularies and methods, and co-authors across groups

agents.name: Center Administrator
agents.description: Manages shared resources, schedules cross-departmental meetings, and handles multi-PI grant administration

memoryArchitecture.description: The center maintains a shared glossary that maps concepts across disciplines, which is continuously updated as new translations are discovered. Each faculty member retains private connections to their home department. The boundary-spanning postdoc has unique access to multiple private knowledge bases, serving as a human bridge. Shared datasets are versioned as different disciplines annotate them with different metadata schemas.

diagram:
graph TD
    CD[Center Director] --> FA[Faculty A - Physics]
    CD --> FB[Faculty B - Biology]
    CD --> FC[Faculty C - CS]
    CD --> BP[Boundary Postdoc]
    CD --> CA[Center Admin]
    FA <-->|collaboration| BP
    FB <-->|collaboration| BP
    FC <-->|collaboration| BP
    DA[Dept A] -.->|tenure line| FA
    DB[Dept B] -.->|tenure line| FB
    DC[Dept C] -.->|tenure line| FC

name: PhD Advisor Tree / Academic Lineage
summary: Knowledge cascades through generations as each PhD advisor trains students who become advisors themselves.
description: The academic lineage is a tree structure where each node is a scholar and edges represent PhD supervision relationships. A senior professor advises several doctoral students, each of whom eventually graduates, starts their own lab, and advises their own students. Intellectual traditions, research methodologies, and even cultural norms propagate down the tree, mutating with each generation. The structure is unique in organizational design because it operates on a generational timescale (5-7 years per edge) and the authority relationship dissolves upon graduation, transforming into a peer or collegial relationship. Some lineages maintain strong identity (the 'Chicago school,' the 'Bourbaki tradition') while others diverge rapidly.
realWorldExample: The Mathematics Genealogy Project traces advisor-student relationships back centuries. Gauss advised Riemann, who advised many students whose lineages produced most of modern mathematics. In machine learning, Geoffrey Hinton's academic tree includes Yann LeCun and Yoshua Bengio through various paths, creating a concentrated lineage that shaped deep learning.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs to propagate specialized knowledge across generations, with each generation refining and extending the tradition while eventually becoming independent peers.
strengths: Deep knowledge transfer through years-long close mentorship relationships
Intellectual traditions accumulate and refine across generations
Graduates become independent agents who extend the tree's reach and diversity
Authority dissolves naturally at graduation, preventing permanent hierarchy
weaknesses: Toxic advisor relationships propagate dysfunctional norms down the tree
Intellectual monoculture can develop within a lineage, creating blind spots
5-7 year cycle time means the tree adapts slowly to paradigm shifts
Prestige of lineage can substitute for quality, creating credentialism

agents.name: Founding Advisor (Generation 0)
agents.description: Established the intellectual tradition; now emeritus or historical figure whose methods and ideas permeate the lineage

agents.name: Generation 1 Advisor A
agents.description: Former student of founder; now leads own lab, adapting the tradition to new domains while maintaining core methodology

agents.name: Generation 1 Advisor B
agents.description: Former student of founder; diverged methodologically but retains intellectual DNA from the founding tradition

agents.name: Generation 2 Student (under A)
agents.description: Current PhD student of Gen 1 Advisor A; learning the refined tradition and beginning to develop independent research direction

agents.name: Generation 2 Student (under B)
agents.description: Current PhD student of Gen 1 Advisor B; inheriting the divergent branch of the tradition

agents.name: Generation 2 Student 2 (under B)
agents.description: Second student of Gen 1 Advisor B; will carry a distinct interpretation of the same tradition

memoryArchitecture.description: The founding advisor's published works form an immutable shared canon that all descendants reference. Each generation adds their own publications, creating a versioned intellectual history. Mentorship norms are transmitted as persistent-private culture within each advisor-student dyad, explaining why branches of the same tree can develop very different lab cultures. The lineage tree itself is a shared append-only data structure maintained by the community.

diagram:
graph TD
    F[Founder Advisor Gen 0] --> A[Advisor A Gen 1]
    F --> B[Advisor B Gen 1]
    A --> SA1[Student A1 Gen 2]
    A --> SA2[Student A2 Gen 2]
    B --> SB1[Student B1 Gen 2]
    B --> SB2[Student B2 Gen 2]
    SA1 -.->|future| GA1[Gen 3...]
    SB1 -.->|future| GB1[Gen 3...]

name: Five Eyes Alliance
summary: Five nations share signals intelligence under bilateral agreements while each retains sovereign collection capabilities.
description: The Five Eyes alliance (FVEY) is a multilateral intelligence-sharing arrangement among the US, UK, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. Each nation operates sovereign intelligence agencies that independently collect signals intelligence (SIGINT), human intelligence (HUMINT), and other forms. Intelligence products are shared according to classification markings and bilateral agreements. Each nation agrees not to spy on the others' citizens (with debated exceptions). The structure is not a hierarchy: no single nation commands the others. Instead, it operates through mutual benefit and division of labor, with each nation covering geographic regions based on historical presence and capability. Disagreements are resolved through diplomatic channels, not command authority.
realWorldExample: NSA (US), GCHQ (UK), CSE (Canada), ASD (Australia), and GCSB (New Zealand) divide global SIGINT coverage. GCHQ covers Europe and the Middle East extensively, ASD covers the Asia-Pacific, and NSA provides the largest technical infrastructure. When GCHQ intercepts communications relevant to US national security, they share the processed intelligence through secure channels marked FVEY, meaning all five nations can access it.
whenToUse: When an agent system involves multiple sovereign peers that must share intelligence or resources without any single agent having command authority over others, while maintaining strict access controls.
strengths: Geographic division of labor provides near-global coverage without any single entity overextending
Mutual agreement not to spy on allies builds deep trust enabling raw intelligence sharing
No single command authority means each nation retains sovereignty over its own operations
Redundant capabilities mean the network survives if one member is compromised
weaknesses: No enforcement mechanism when a member violates agreements (e.g., spying on allies)
Classification barriers can prevent timely sharing of urgent intelligence
Smallest members (NZ) have limited leverage against largest (US) despite nominal equality
Shared infrastructure creates systemic risk if any member agency is breached

agents.name: NSA (United States)
agents.description: Largest technical SIGINT capability; provides backbone infrastructure, satellite collection, and the majority of processed intelligence volume

agents.name: GCHQ (United Kingdom)
agents.description: Major SIGINT capability with extensive European and Middle Eastern coverage; deep historical tradecraft and cable-tapping infrastructure

agents.name: CSE (Canada)
agents.description: Covers Arctic, Northern Atlantic, and portions of Asia; provides cyber operations capability and linguistic expertise

agents.name: ASD (Australia)
agents.description: Primary coverage of Asia-Pacific and Indian Ocean region; hosts joint facilities and provides regional human intelligence networks

agents.name: GCSB (New Zealand)
agents.description: Covers South Pacific and contributes satellite intercept capability; smallest member but provides critical geographic coverage

agents.name: Liaison Officers
agents.description: Embedded staff at partner agencies who facilitate real-time intelligence sharing and resolve classification issues

memoryArchitecture.description: Each nation maintains a private intelligence store that is never shared, containing sources, methods, and nationally sensitive material. A shared FVEY-classified tier contains processed intelligence products available to all five partners. Bilateral channels exist between specific pairs for material too sensitive for full five-way sharing. Liaison officers have unique cross-access that bridges private and shared tiers. Classification markings (FVEY, bilateral, national-only) control memory access at every level.

diagram:
graph TD
    NSA[NSA - United States] <-->|bilateral| GCHQ[GCHQ - United Kingdom]
    NSA <-->|bilateral| CSE[CSE - Canada]
    NSA <-->|bilateral| ASD[ASD - Australia]
    NSA <-->|bilateral| GCSB[GCSB - New Zealand]
    GCHQ <-->|bilateral| ASD
    GCHQ <-->|bilateral| CSE
    FVEY[FVEY Shared Pool] --- NSA
    FVEY --- GCHQ
    FVEY --- CSE
    FVEY --- ASD
    FVEY --- GCSB

name: Regulatory Agency (FDA/SEC Model)
summary: One agency writes rules, investigates violations, and adjudicates cases under one specialized roof.
description: Regulatory agencies like the FDA or SEC are sometimes called the 'fourth branch of government' because they combine functions normally separated. The rulemaking division drafts regulations through notice-and-comment procedures, incorporating public input. The enforcement division investigates potential violations, conducts examinations, and brings enforcement actions. The adjudication division (administrative law judges) hears contested cases. Commissioners appointed by the president and confirmed by the Senate set policy direction. This concentration of powers enables specialized expertise and faster action than Congress, but raises due process concerns. Internal firewalls between divisions prevent the same people who write rules from adjudicating violations of those rules.
realWorldExample: The SEC's Division of Enforcement investigates insider trading, then refers cases to the Commission for formal charges. The accused can settle or contest the charges before an SEC Administrative Law Judge, whose decision can be appealed to the full Commission, then to federal courts. Meanwhile, the Division of Corporation Finance reviews filings and the Division of Trading and Markets writes rules for exchanges.
whenToUse: When an agent system must combine standard-setting, monitoring, and enforcement into a single specialized entity, with internal firewalls to prevent conflicts between these functions.
strengths: Deep domain specialization impossible for generalist legislators or courts to achieve
Speed of rulemaking and enforcement compared to congressional legislation and federal litigation
Notice-and-comment rulemaking incorporates stakeholder input systematically
Internal firewalls between divisions maintain procedural fairness
weaknesses: Concentration of powers raises due process concerns about being judge, jury, and rulemaker
Regulatory capture occurs when the agency identifies more with industry than public interest
Political appointee commissioners create policy instability across administrations
Rulemaking through enforcement actions (regulation by litigation) bypasses democratic input

agents.name: Commission Chair
agents.description: Presidentially appointed; sets policy priorities, schedules votes on rules and enforcement actions, and represents the agency publicly

agents.name: Commissioner A
agents.description: One of the appointed commissioners; votes on rules and enforcement actions, may dissent publicly

agents.name: Commissioner B
agents.description: Another appointed commissioner providing an independent vote and perspective on agency actions

agents.name: Director of Rulemaking
agents.description: Drafts proposed rules, manages notice-and-comment periods, analyzes public input, and prepares final rules for commission vote

agents.name: Director of Enforcement
agents.description: Investigates potential violations, authorizes examinations, and recommends enforcement actions to the commission

agents.name: Administrative Law Judge
agents.description: Independently adjudicates contested enforcement actions; issues initial decisions that can be appealed to the full commission

agents.name: General Counsel
agents.description: Provides legal advice to the commission, defends agency actions in federal court, and ensures procedural compliance

memoryArchitecture.description: Published rules and enforcement actions are shared public memory. Ongoing investigations are strictly private to enforcement, firewalled from rulemaking to prevent prejudging. ALJ deliberations are private and independent from the commission until a decision is issued. Public comments are shared inputs that become part of the rulemaking record. Commissioner votes are recorded but deliberations may be private under the sunshine act exemptions.

diagram:
graph TD
    CC[Commission Chair] --> CA[Commissioner A]
    CC --> CB[Commissioner B]
    CC --> RD[Rulemaking Director]
    CC --> ED[Enforcement Director]
    CC --> GC[General Counsel]
    RD -->|proposed rules| PUB[Public Comment]
    PUB -->|comments| RD
    ED -->|investigations| TARGETS[Regulated Entities]
    ED -->|charges| ALJ[Admin Law Judge]
    ALJ -->|appeal| CC
    GC -->|legal review| RD
    GC -->|legal review| ED

name: International Criminal Court
summary: Prosecution, defense, judges, and victim representatives interact in a treaty-based court for crimes against humanity.
description: The International Criminal Court (ICC) operates under the Rome Statute to prosecute genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes, and aggression. The Office of the Prosecutor independently investigates and brings cases. Pre-Trial, Trial, and Appeals Chambers of judges evaluate evidence and render verdicts. Uniquely, the ICC includes a Victims Participation Unit that gives victims legal standing to present views and concerns separate from the prosecution, a feature absent from most national courts. The Registry provides administrative support and manages witness protection. The structure must navigate tensions between state sovereignty (states can refuse to cooperate), prosecutorial independence, and the rights of both victims and accused.
realWorldExample: In the prosecution of Thomas Lubanga for conscripting child soldiers in the DRC, the ICC's Office of the Prosecutor gathered evidence from conflict zones while the Victims Participation Unit enabled 129 victims to participate through legal representatives. The Trial Chamber heard testimony over three years. Lubanga's defense team challenged evidence provenance. The Appeals Chamber later addressed novel questions about victim reparations.
whenToUse: When an agent system must adjudicate disputes involving multiple sovereign stakeholders, with independent investigation, adversarial proceedings, and explicit representation of affected parties beyond the two primary adversaries.
strengths: Victim participation gives affected parties a voice beyond just serving as prosecution witnesses
Prosecutorial independence from political bodies protects against selective justice
Three-chamber structure (Pre-Trial, Trial, Appeals) provides layered review of decisions
Treaty basis gives the court legitimacy derived from state consent rather than coercion
weaknesses: No enforcement mechanism: relies on state cooperation for arrests, evidence, and sentences
Cases take years or decades, during which evidence degrades and witnesses face danger
Perception of bias toward prosecuting African conflicts undermines universal legitimacy
Major powers (US, China, Russia) not party to the Rome Statute, limiting jurisdiction

agents.name: Chief Prosecutor
agents.description: Independently investigates situations, gathers evidence from conflict zones, and brings charges before the Pre-Trial Chamber

agents.name: Pre-Trial Chamber Judge
agents.description: Reviews prosecution evidence to decide whether charges are confirmed and the case proceeds to trial

agents.name: Trial Chamber Presiding Judge
agents.description: Presides over the trial, rules on evidence admissibility, and renders verdict and sentence

agents.name: Defense Counsel
agents.description: Represents the accused; challenges evidence, cross-examines witnesses, and argues for acquittal or mitigation

agents.name: Victims' Legal Representative
agents.description: Represents victims' views and concerns in proceedings; can present evidence and question witnesses on victims' behalf

agents.name: Registrar
agents.description: Manages court administration, witness protection programs, detention facilities, and outreach to affected communities

agents.name: Appeals Chamber Judge
agents.description: Reviews trial decisions for legal errors; can overturn convictions, modify sentences, or order new trials

shared: Rome Statute and Rules of Procedure
memoryArchitecture.description: The Rome Statute is the immutable shared foundation. Court transcripts are shared but can be partially redacted for witness protection. The prosecution and defense maintain strictly private evidence files that are disclosed to each other only according to disclosure rules. Victim identities may be kept private even from the accused in extreme cases. Judgments are versioned through the appeals process, with each chamber's decision building on but potentially overturning the previous.

diagram:
graph TD
    OTP[Office of the Prosecutor] -->|charges| PTC[Pre-Trial Chamber]
    PTC -->|confirms charges| TC[Trial Chamber]
    DC[Defense Counsel] --> PTC
    DC --> TC
    VR[Victims Representative] --> PTC
    VR --> TC
    TC -->|verdict| AC[Appeals Chamber]
    REG[Registrar] -->|admin support| TC
    REG -->|witness protection| OTP
    REG -->|witness protection| DC

name: Coding Bootcamp Cohort
summary: Career changers learn intensively in a time-boxed cohort with pair programming and a demo day capstone.
description: A coding bootcamp compresses years of traditional education into 12-16 weeks of intensive, full-time instruction. Students form a cohort that progresses together through a structured curriculum. Lead instructors deliver core content while teaching assistants provide one-on-one debugging help. Mentors from industry offer career guidance and code review. The curriculum is project-based: students build increasingly complex applications, culminating in a capstone project presented at a public demo day. Career services coaches prepare students for job interviews through mock technical screens, resume workshops, and employer networking. The cohort model creates intense peer bonds and accountability, but the compressed timeline means gaps in fundamentals are common.
realWorldExample: A Hack Reactor cohort of 30 students spends 6 days a week for 13 weeks in an immersive JavaScript program. The first 6 weeks cover fundamentals with daily pair programming rotations. Weeks 7-9 are group projects with sprints and standups mimicking real development teams. Weeks 10-13 are solo capstone projects. Demo day brings in 50 hiring partners. Career services continues for 6 months post-graduation.
whenToUse: When an agent system must rapidly train new agents from novice to functional competence in a specific domain, using intense time-boxed immersion with peer learning and mentorship.
strengths: Compressed timeline forces focus and eliminates the procrastination possible in longer programs
Cohort model creates peer accountability and collaborative learning culture
Project-based curriculum produces portfolio evidence that flat credential-checking cannot
Industry mentors and career services directly connect learning to employment outcomes
weaknesses: Speed sacrifices depth: graduates may lack theoretical foundations for novel problems
One-size-fits-all pacing leaves faster students bored and slower students overwhelmed
Demo day pressure can incentivize impressive-looking projects over solid engineering
High cost and intensity creates survivorship bias in reported outcomes

agents.name: Lead Instructor
agents.description: Delivers core curriculum through lectures and live coding; sets daily learning objectives and designs project specifications

agents.name: Teaching Assistant
agents.description: Provides one-on-one debugging help, reviews student code, and identifies students who are falling behind for early intervention

agents.name: Industry Mentor
agents.description: Working software engineer who volunteers for weekly code reviews, career advice, and mock technical interviews

agents.name: Cohort Student (Strong)
agents.description: A student with some prior experience who progresses quickly and often helps peers during pair programming

agents.name: Cohort Student (Struggling)
agents.description: A career changer with no prior coding experience who needs extra TA support and repetition to solidify concepts

agents.name: Career Services Coach
agents.description: Prepares students for job search through resume workshops, mock interviews, salary negotiation training, and employer introductions

agents.name: Program Manager
agents.description: Manages cohort logistics, tracks student progress metrics, handles student welfare issues, and coordinates demo day

memoryArchitecture.description: The curriculum is a shared, versioned document that instructors update between cohorts based on industry trends and student feedback. Student code repositories are shared within the cohort for peer learning. Progress metrics are private between the student and program manager to avoid public shaming. Career services maintains private job search pipelines for each graduate. The demo day presentation archive becomes shared institutional memory that attracts future employers and students.

diagram:
graph TD
    PM[Program Manager] --> LI[Lead Instructor]
    PM --> CC[Career Coach]
    LI --> TA[Teaching Assistant]
    LI -->|lectures| SA[Student A]
    LI -->|lectures| SB[Student B]
    TA -->|1-on-1 help| SA
    TA -->|1-on-1 help| SB
    SA <-->|pair programming| SB
    IM[Industry Mentor] -->|code review| SA
    IM -->|code review| SB
    CC -->|career prep| SA
    CC -->|career prep| SB

name: Military War College Seminar
summary: Senior officers develop strategic thinking through Socratic discussion, case studies, and wargames.
description: War college seminars educate colonels and generals for strategic-level leadership. Unlike traditional military training with its rigid hierarchy, seminars use the Socratic method: a faculty seminar leader poses questions and facilitates debate rather than lecturing. Students are peers of roughly equal rank who bring decades of operational experience. The curriculum covers strategy, history, policy, and joint operations through case studies, wargames, and research papers. Students are organized into small seminar groups (12-16) that stay together for the full academic year, developing trust that enables candid discussion. The deliberate flattening of hierarchy within the seminar room contrasts sharply with the rank-conscious culture these officers inhabit outside it.
realWorldExample: At the US Army War College in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, a seminar of 16 colonels from different branches (Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines, allied nations) spends a year together. The faculty seminar leader, a retired general or PhD strategist, poses a scenario: 'China blockades Taiwan. You advise the President. What do you recommend?' Two hours of structured debate follow, drawing on history, doctrine, and personal operational experience.
whenToUse: When an agent system must develop strategic reasoning in already-experienced agents through facilitated debate and case analysis, rather than top-down instruction.
strengths: Socratic method develops critical thinking rather than rote knowledge absorption
Peer learning leverages decades of diverse operational experience across branches and nations
Year-long seminar cohort builds trust that enables genuine intellectual challenge
Deliberate hierarchy flattening allows colonels to challenge generals' assumptions safely
weaknesses: Small seminar size limits the diversity of perspectives available in any single discussion
Socratic facilitation requires exceptionally skilled faculty who can guide without dominating
Military cultural deference to rank can reassert itself despite structural flattening
Strategic concepts are harder to assess than tactical skills, making evaluation subjective

agents.name: Faculty Seminar Leader
agents.description: Retired senior officer or civilian strategist who facilitates Socratic discussion, poses scenarios, and guides analysis without imposing conclusions

agents.name: Army Colonel
agents.description: Brings ground force operational experience; contributes perspectives on land warfare, counterinsurgency, and force deployment

agents.name: Navy Captain
agents.description: Brings maritime and carrier operations experience; contributes perspectives on sea control, power projection, and naval strategy

agents.name: Air Force Colonel
agents.description: Brings air superiority and space operations experience; contributes perspectives on air power theory and strategic bombing

agents.name: Marine Colonel
agents.description: Brings amphibious and expeditionary warfare experience; bridges land and naval perspectives

agents.name: Allied Nation Officer
agents.description: International fellow who brings a different national strategic perspective, challenging US-centric assumptions

agents.name: Civilian Professor
agents.description: Academic expert in strategy, history, or political science who provides theoretical frameworks and historical context

memoryArchitecture.description: The shared case study library and doctrine repository provide common reference points for discussion. Each officer brings private operational experience, some of which is classified and can only be alluded to rather than shared directly. The seminar leader maintains persistent notes on discussion themes and student development. Wargame outcomes are shared learning artifacts. Individual research papers are private until presentation. The year-long shared experience creates a persistent peer network that extends well beyond graduation.

diagram:
graph TD
    SL[Seminar Leader] --> SD[Seminar Discussion]
    CP[Civilian Professor] --> SD
    AC[Army Colonel] --> SD
    NC[Navy Captain] --> SD
    AFC[Air Force Colonel] --> SD
    MC[Marine Colonel] --> SD
    AO[Allied Officer] --> SD
    SD --> WG[Wargame Exercise]
    SD --> RP[Research Papers]
    WG --> AAR[After Action Review]

name: Flocking V-Formation
summary: Birds rotate the lead position to share wind-breaking costs while followers draft in formation.
description: In V-formation flight, the lead bird breaks the headwind while trailing birds draft in the upwash from the leader's wingtips, reducing their energy expenditure by up to 70%. The key organizational insight is that leadership rotates: when the lead bird tires, it drops back to a trailing position and another bird moves forward. No bird is permanently in charge. Each bird follows two simple rules: maintain position relative to the bird ahead, and match the flock's speed. The V-shape emerges from these local rules without centralized coordination. Communication is through physical position and wing-beat synchronization rather than explicit signaling. The formation automatically adapts to wind conditions and flock size.
realWorldExample: Migrating Canada geese fly in V-formation across thousands of miles. Research using GPS loggers on northern bald ibises showed that birds precisely positioned their wingtips in the upwash zone of the bird ahead, and that leadership rotated in reciprocal pairs: birds that led more were followed more, creating a cooperative equilibrium without explicit negotiation.
whenToUse: When an agent system involves a shared costly task where the lead agent bears disproportionate burden, requiring fair rotation of leadership to prevent burnout while maintaining collective efficiency.
strengths: Leadership rotation prevents any single agent from burning out on the hardest work
Drafting effect means the system achieves more total work than individual agents working alone
No centralized coordination needed: formation emerges from simple local rules
Formation automatically scales with more agents and adapts to environmental conditions
weaknesses: Requires all agents to be roughly equally capable of leading (no specialist leader)
Rotation fairness can break down if some agents free-ride in trailing positions
Formation is fragile: if one bird breaks position, downstream birds lose efficiency
Only works for tasks with a clear 'headwind' metaphor where leading is costly but following is efficient

agents.name: Current Lead Bird
agents.description: Currently breaking headwind at the point of the V; bears highest energy cost but sets pace and direction for the flock

agents.name: Left Wing Position 1
agents.description: First bird on the left wing; drafts off lead bird's right wingtip upwash and is next in rotation to lead

agents.name: Right Wing Position 1
agents.description: First bird on the right wing; drafts off lead bird's left wingtip upwash and monitors for crosswind shifts

agents.name: Left Wing Position 2
agents.description: Second bird on the left wing; maintains position relative to wing-left-1 and enjoys maximum energy savings

agents.name: Right Wing Position 2
agents.description: Second bird on the right wing; maintains position relative to wing-right-1

agents.name: Tail Bird
agents.description: Rearmost position; recently rotated from lead and is recovering energy while drafting behind the entire formation

memoryArchitecture.description: Memory is almost entirely ephemeral and positional. Each bird's state is defined by its current position in the formation and its energy level. The shared state is simply the flock's heading and speed, communicated through physical proximity rather than explicit messages. The rotation schedule is emergent: birds self-assess their fatigue and initiate position swaps. No persistent memory of past flights is needed because the rules are embedded in behavior, not in stored data.

diagram:
graph TD
    LB[Lead Bird] --> WL1[Left Wing 1]
    LB --> WR1[Right Wing 1]
    WL1 --> WL2[Left Wing 2]
    WR1 --> WR2[Right Wing 2]
    WL2 --> TB[Tail Bird]
    TB -.->|rotation| LB
    WL1 -.->|rotation| LB
    WR1 -.->|rotation| LB

name: Jellyfish Nerve Net
summary: A decentralized nervous system with no brain coordinates behavior through local signal propagation across a nerve net.
description: Jellyfish possess a nerve net rather than a centralized brain. Neurons are distributed evenly throughout the body, connected in a diffuse network. When one part of the body detects a stimulus (food, predator, light), the signal propagates through the nerve net in all directions. The body responds as a whole through coordinated muscle contractions, but no single neuron or cluster acts as a controller. Rhopalia (sensory structures around the bell margin) detect light and gravity, providing directional input that biases the net's activity without commanding it. This architecture makes the organism remarkably resilient: any portion of the nerve net can be removed and the rest continues functioning. The trade-off is limited behavioral complexity.
realWorldExample: The box jellyfish Tripedalia cystophora has 24 eyes distributed across four rhopalia, but no brain to process visual information centrally. Instead, each rhopalium processes its own visual input and modulates local swimming contractions. The jellyfish navigates toward light and avoids obstacles through this distributed processing, achieving surprisingly sophisticated behavior without any central coordination.
whenToUse: When an agent system must be maximally resilient to partial failure, with every node capable of both sensing and acting, and where simple reactive behavior is sufficient rather than complex planning.
strengths: Maximum resilience: any portion can be destroyed and the system continues functioning
No single point of failure because there is no central controller to lose
Extremely low latency for local responses since signals don't need to travel to a brain
Scales naturally: adding more nerve net extends capability without architectural changes
weaknesses: Cannot perform complex sequential reasoning or planning without central processing
Signal propagation in all directions wastes energy on irrelevant body regions
No learning or memory formation capability limits behavioral adaptation
Conflicting stimuli from multiple sources cannot be prioritized or arbitrated

agents.name: Nerve Node Alpha
agents.description: A cluster of neurons in one body region that senses local stimuli and propagates signals to adjacent nodes

agents.name: Nerve Node Beta
agents.description: Adjacent nerve cluster that receives signals from Alpha and propagates them further while triggering local muscle contraction

agents.name: Nerve Node Gamma
agents.description: Another nerve cluster in a different body region, independently sensing and responding to local conditions

agents.name: Nerve Node Delta
agents.description: Nerve cluster on the opposite side of the body, receiving attenuated signals and responding proportionally

agents.name: Rhopalium (Sensory Organ)
agents.description: Marginal sensory structure with light-sensing and gravity-sensing cells that biases nerve net activity directionally

agents.name: Rhopalium (Secondary)
agents.description: Second sensory organ on the opposite margin, providing redundant directional sensing

memoryArchitecture.description: There is essentially no persistent memory in this system. All state is ephemeral: signals propagate, trigger responses, and dissipate. The rhopalia provide continuous sensory input but do not store it. The nerve net has no mechanism for learning or remembering past events. Coordination emerges entirely from the physics of signal propagation speed and the geometry of the nerve net. This is the most purely reactive architecture possible.

diagram:
graph TD
    R1[Rhopalium 1] -->|light/gravity| NN1[Nerve Node Alpha]
    R2[Rhopalium 2] -->|light/gravity| NN3[Nerve Node Gamma]
    NN1 <-->|signal| NN2[Nerve Node Beta]
    NN2 <-->|signal| NN3
    NN3 <-->|signal| NN4[Nerve Node Delta]
    NN4 <-->|signal| NN1
    NN1 -->|contract| M1[Muscle Region 1]
    NN2 -->|contract| M2[Muscle Region 2]
    NN3 -->|contract| M3[Muscle Region 3]
    NN4 -->|contract| M4[Muscle Region 4]

name: East India Company
summary: A chartered corporation wielding sovereign powers: armies, currency, and territorial governance for profit.
description: The British East India Company (EIC) was a corporate-state hybrid that operated from 1600 to 1874. Chartered by the Crown, it held monopoly trading rights but gradually acquired sovereign functions: raising armies, administering justice, collecting taxes, and conducting diplomacy. The Court of Directors in London set strategy, but the vast distance to India (6+ months by ship) meant that presidencies in Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay operated with enormous autonomy. Governors-general made war and peace decisions without London's approval. The Company maintained separate military, commercial, and administrative hierarchies that frequently conflicted. This structure produced enormous wealth but also catastrophic governance failures (the Bengal Famine of 1770), eventually leading Parliament to progressively strip the Company of its powers.
realWorldExample: In 1757, Robert Clive led Company troops to victory at the Battle of Plassey, effectively conquering Bengal. He made himself governor and the Company became tax collector for 30 million people. The Court of Directors in London learned of these events months later. Clive's commercial agents simultaneously traded in the region they governed, creating massive conflicts of interest. By 1773, Parliament passed the Regulating Act to impose government oversight.
whenToUse: When an agent system must operate autonomously at great distance from its principals, combining commercial, military, and governance functions, with the understanding that this concentration of power creates severe accountability risks.
strengths: Extreme autonomy enables rapid local adaptation without waiting for distant approval
Combining trade, military, and governance creates self-reinforcing power in new territories
Chartered monopoly provides stable funding and legal authority for long-term operations
Decentralized presidencies can pursue region-specific strategies simultaneously
weaknesses: Communication lag means principals cannot monitor or correct agent behavior in real time
Combining commercial incentives with governance power creates catastrophic conflicts of interest
Military force amplifies commercial disputes into wars that serve profit, not public interest
Accountability gap between London directors and India operations enables systematic exploitation

agents.name: Court of Directors
agents.description: London-based board that sets overall strategy, allocates capital, appoints governors, and reports to shareholders and Parliament

agents.name: Governor-General of India
agents.description: Supreme authority in India; makes military, diplomatic, and administrative decisions with months of autonomy between London communications

agents.name: Presidency Governor (Madras/Bombay)
agents.description: Governs a regional presidency with its own army, revenue collection, and trade operations; nominally subordinate to Governor-General

agents.name: Military Commander
agents.description: Commands Company sepoy and European troops; conducts campaigns, garrisons forts, and enforces Company authority by force

agents.name: Chief Merchant (Commercial)
agents.description: Manages trading operations, negotiates with local rulers for trade concessions, and operates warehouses and shipping

agents.name: Revenue Collector (Diwani)
agents.description: Collects land taxes from Indian subjects in Company-controlled territories; the primary funding source for military and operations

agents.name: Crown Representative / Board of Control
agents.description: Parliamentary oversight agent imposed after 1784; reviews political and military decisions but not commercial ones

private: local treaty terms with Indian rulers
personal trade accounts of Company servants
memoryArchitecture.description: The 6-month communication lag between London and India created a de facto split-brain architecture. The Court of Directors maintained one version of reality based on dispatches, while governors maintained another based on ground truth. Revenue records were private and frequently manipulated. Company servants maintained personal trade accounts that often conflicted with Company interests. Parliamentary oversight created a third memory layer that occasionally audited the gap between London's and India's versions of events.

diagram:
graph TD
    PARL[Parliament] -->|oversight| COD[Court of Directors - London]
    COD -->|dispatches 6mo| GG[Governor-General - India]
    GG --> PMad[Presidency - Madras]
    GG --> PBom[Presidency - Bombay]
    GG --> MIL[Military Commander]
    GG --> CM[Chief Merchant]
    GG --> RC[Revenue Collector]
    MIL -->|force| TERR[Territories]
    RC -->|taxes| TERR
    CM -->|trade| TERR

name: Venetian Republic
summary: Overlapping councils, random selection, and term limits prevented power seizure for 1,100 years.
description: The Republic of Venice (697-1797) was history's longest-lasting republic, surviving 1,100 years through an elaborate system of checks against power concentration. The Doge served as head of state for life but had almost no independent power, acting only with council approval. The Great Council (all patrician families) elected officials through a unique multi-round process alternating between random lottery and voting, making bribery nearly impossible. The Council of Ten handled state security with extraordinary powers but rotating membership and term limits. The Senate managed foreign policy and trade. Three Avogadori (state attorneys) could challenge any government action as unconstitutional. Anonymous denunciation boxes (bocca di leone) enabled citizens to report corruption. This layered system deliberately traded efficiency for stability.
realWorldExample: Electing a Doge required 11 rounds alternating between lottery and ballot. Starting with the Great Council of ~2,000 members, 30 were chosen by lot, reduced to 9 by lot, who elected 40, reduced to 12 by lot, who elected 25, reduced to 9 by lot, who elected 45, reduced to 11 by lot, who elected 41 final electors. These 41 then elected the Doge by supermajority. This process made it virtually impossible to rig the outcome.
whenToUse: When an agent system must prevent any single agent or coalition from accumulating unchecked power, prioritizing long-term institutional stability over short-term decisional efficiency.
strengths: Multi-round lottery-ballot hybrid makes election manipulation virtually impossible
Overlapping councils with different compositions prevent any single body from dominating
Term limits and rotation ensure fresh perspectives and prevent power entrenchment
1,100 years of stability validates the architecture's resilience to internal threats
weaknesses: Extreme procedural complexity makes rapid decision-making nearly impossible in crises
Restricted to patrician class: stability came at the cost of excluding most citizens from power
Anonymous denunciation system was vulnerable to abuse for personal vendettas
Institutional conservatism that preserved the republic also prevented necessary adaptation to modernity

agents.name: Doge
agents.description: Ceremonial head of state elected for life; presides over councils but cannot act unilaterally, representing the republic's continuity and dignity

agents.name: Great Council
agents.description: Assembly of all patrician families (~2,000 members) that elects officials, passes legislation, and serves as the ultimate sovereign body

agents.name: Senate (Pregadi)
agents.description: Deliberative body of ~300 members managing foreign policy, trade agreements, and military strategy

agents.name: Council of Ten
agents.description: State security body with extraordinary powers; investigates conspiracies, manages intelligence, and can act in secret, but members rotate annually

agents.name: Avogadori di Comun
agents.description: Three state attorneys who review all government actions for constitutionality and can block or reverse illegal decisions

agents.name: Ducal Councilors (Minor Council)
agents.description: Six councilors who must be present for any Doge action to be valid; serve as a constant check on ducal authority

agents.name: State Inquisitors
agents.description: Three officials drawn from the Council of Ten with power to investigate and prosecute treason, operating in total secrecy

memoryArchitecture.description: Venice's memory architecture was deliberately fragmented to prevent power concentration. Constitutional laws were shared and immutable without Great Council supermajority. The Council of Ten maintained private security files but with rotating access as membership changed annually. The Doge's actions were recorded in shared minutes that required Ducal Councilor co-signatures. Anonymous denunciations were private to the Council of Ten and could only be acted upon if corroborated by independent investigation. Election records used a lottery-based system that was intentionally unrecordable to prevent pattern analysis.

diagram:
graph TD
    GC[Great Council 2000+] -->|elects| DOGE[Doge]
    GC -->|elects| SEN[Senate 300]
    GC -->|elects| C10[Council of Ten]
    DOGE --> DC[Ducal Councilors 6]
    DC -->|must approve| DOGE
    C10 --> INQ[State Inquisitors 3]
    AVG[Avogadori 3] -->|constitutional review| GC
    AVG -->|constitutional review| SEN
    AVG -->|constitutional review| C10
    BOCCA[Bocca di Leone] -->|denunciations| C10
